turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kennedy has the opinion of the court in case 17-155, Hughes v. United States.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: A federal statute provides that in certain instances a prisoner who is serving a sentence is eligible for a sentence reduction when the Sentencing Commission has lowered the sentencing guidelines. The eligibility extends to a prisoner who has been sentenced to a term of imprisonment based on a sentencing range that has subsequently been lowered by the Sentencing Commission and that statute is 18 U.S.C. §3582(c)(2). The question here is whether the sentence imposed on the prisoner in this case was based on the guidelines when he entered his guilty plea under an agreement with the prosecution that is called a Type-C plea agreement and that's where the defendant and the government agree that the defendant will plead guilty and then serve a particular sentence and the Type-C agreement that they agree on must be then approved by the trial judge. Seven terms ago in Freeman v. United States this Court considered the same question that is involved here, that is, whether the sentence under Type-C agreement is based on the guidelines. In Freeman no single interpretation or rationale commanded a majority of this Court. Now Marks v. United States was a case decided in 1977 and Marks discussed what other court should do when this Court has separate opinions addressing an issue but when no single rationale commands a majority. So, when confronted with the separate opinions on the sentencing question presented in Freeman various Courts of Appeals consulted Marks to determine which Freeman opinion to follow and they reached different conclusions, and in today's case the Court had asked the parties to address the Marks question as a threshold issue. After consideration of the sentencing issue first discussed in Freeman and now presented again here, the Court in today's case now has a majority with respect to that substantive sentencing issue. So it's unnecessary to discuss how Marks should be interpreted. The Court holds that a defendant who entered a Type-C agreement is eligible for sentence reduction under 3582(c) so long as the defendant's sentencing range was part of the framework the District Court used in imposing a sentence or accepting the agreement. In the typical sentencing case there will be no question of the defendant's guideline range was a basis for his sentence, for the guidelines are the starting point for every sentencing calculation in the federal system. A sentence imposed pursuant to a Type-C agreement is no exception to this general rule, although the government and the defendant may agree to a specific sentence that bargain is contingent on the District Court's accepting the agreement and the guidelines prohibit courts from accepting a Type-C agreement without first evaluating the recommended sentence in light of the defendant's guideline range. So in the usual case a sentence imposed pursuant to the agreement is based on the defendant's guideline range. This interpretation furthers the purpose of the Sentencing Act. The Act aims to create a comprehensive sentencing scheme in which those who commit crimes of similar severity under similar conditions receive similar sentences. In this case the District Court accepted petitioner Eric Hughes' Type-C agreement and it imposed a 180-month sentence after concluding that the sentence was consistent with the guidelines. Thus the guideline range was a basis for the sentence that the District Court imposed. That range has subsequently been lowered by the Sentencing Commission so Hughes is eligible for relief under the section. The Court expresses no view as to whether the District Court should exercise its discretion to reduce Hughes' sentence after considering the relevant statutory factors and the commission's policy statements. The judgment of the Court of Appeals is reversed. The case is remanded for further proceedings consistent with this opinion. Justice Sotomayor has filed a concurring opinion. The Chief Justice has filed a dissenting opinion in which Justices Thomas and Alito have joined.
